questions.scenario:
A feature branch push does not start CI. Opening a pull request from that branch into main does start it. The workflow contains:

~~~yaml
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
~~~

Which explanation matches the event filters?

options.text: The workflow definition from the feature branch is ignored for `push`, but the base branch definition handles the pull request.
Push filters the updated branch; pull_request filters the target branch, which is main.
A repository ruleset suppresses feature-branch push workflows but requires the same workflow as a pull request check.
The `push` filter evaluates the feature branch, while `pull_request` evaluates the source branch after it is compared with `main`.

questions.explanation: The same `branches` key applies to different event data: `push` selects the pushed branch, while `pull_request` selects the base branch. The feature push did not match, but the pull request into `main` did. No repository-ruleset evidence is needed to explain the behavior, and the source branch is not what this pull request filter evaluates. See [GitHub Actions events](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/workflows-and-actions/events-that-trigger-workflows).

questions.scenario: The test job creates `coverage/report.xml`. A later package job declares `needs: test` but cannot find that file. Both jobs use `ubuntu-latest`, and compliance requires the package job to consume the report produced by this exact workflow run rather than a value restored from an earlier run. What design change is required?

options.text: Cache `coverage/report.xml` in the test job and restore the same cache key in package.
Run package on the same runner label and set `needs: test` so GitHub reuses the completed runner.
Write the report to a repository-level environment variable and reconstruct the file in package.
Upload the report or required build output from test and download it in package, because jobs use separate runners.

questions.explanation: `needs` controls ordering and success dependency, not filesystem sharing. An artifact is designed to transfer a specific run output between jobs. A cache is optimized for reusable dependencies and can be stale or immutable under a key. Runner labels do not preserve a hosted machine, and environment variables are unsuitable for a report file. See [GitHub-hosted runner execution](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/manage-runners/github-hosted-runners/use-github-hosted-runners).

questions.scenario: A monorepo test workflow should run for application changes and for changes to its own definition, but changes to other services' workflow files must not start this service's expensive suite. The current paths filter includes src/** and package-lock.json, so a pull request that changes only .github/workflows/test.yml never exercises the new CI logic. Which review change is best?

options.text: Add `.github/workflows/**` to the filter so any workflow change runs every monorepo test suite.
Create a second workflow triggered only by changes under `.github/workflows/**` and leave the current filter unchanged.
Add the workflow file path to the pull request paths filter.
Use `paths-ignore` for documentation and rely on the existing positive paths to include workflow files implicitly.

questions.explanation: Adding this workflow's exact path preserves the intended application scope and validates changes to the automation contract. A broad workflow glob may trigger unrelated monorepo suites. A second workflow can work, but it duplicates the test contract and does not directly fix this workflow's skip. Positive `paths` entries do not gain implicit files from `paths-ignore`. See [GitHub workflow syntax for path filters](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/workflows-and-actions/workflow-syntax#onpushpull_requestpull_request_targetpathspaths-ignore).

questions.scenario: A compatibility matrix tests Node 20 and 22 on Ubuntu and Windows. The first Windows job fails, and GitHub cancels other in-progress matrix jobs. The team wants the complete compatibility picture from every variant. What should change?

options.text: Set `continue-on-error: true` on every matrix job so all variants finish and the workflow reports their results.
Mark the Windows matrix entry as experimental with `continue-on-error`, leaving fail-fast enabled for other variants.
Set strategy.fail-fast to false for the matrix.
Increase `max-parallel` so all matrix jobs start before the first failure can cancel queued variants.

questions.explanation: Matrix `fail-fast: false` preserves normal failure status while allowing every combination to finish. Setting `continue-on-error` everywhere hides required failures. Marking only Windows experimental changes the acceptance policy, and higher parallelism cannot guarantee every job starts before a failure. See [GitHub matrix strategies](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/write-workflows/choose-what-workflows-do/run-job-variations).

questions.scenario: Pull request checks should cancel when a newer commit arrives, but production deployments must finish rather than be interrupted. Which concurrency design fits both workloads?

options.text: Cancel stale pull request groups, but queue production groups without cancellation.
Use separate pull request and production groups, with cancellation enabled for both to keep only the newest commit.
Cancel stale pull request runs, but give each production commit a unique group so deployments can run concurrently.
Use one service-level group with cancellation disabled, so pull request checks and deployments queue safely.

questions.explanation: Stale pull request checks can be discarded, but interrupting or overlapping production deployments can leave uncertain state. Distinct groups with cancellation only for pull requests express those risks. Unique production groups permit overlap, while one shared queue delays checks behind deployments and fails the stale-check requirement. See [GitHub Actions concurrency](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/how-tos/write-workflows/choose-when-workflows-run/control-workflow-concurrency).

questions.scenario: A compliance workflow audits production automatically every night. During an incident, an authorized engineer must also be able to launch the same workflow for either staging or production and must explicitly choose the target environment. Which trigger and data choices belong in the workflow?

options.text: A reusable `workflow_call` trigger with the schedule passed as a string input.
A schedule trigger with a cron expression for the nightly run.
A `repository_dispatch` trigger for the manual run, with the environment read from `github.event.client_payload`.
A workflow_dispatch trigger with a declared input read through the inputs context.
A `workflow_dispatch` trigger with an environment variable declared at workflow scope rather than an input.

questions.explanation: `schedule` creates time-based runs, while `workflow_dispatch` adds the Run workflow UI and declared inputs. `repository_dispatch` is useful for external API callers but does not provide the requested native manual interface. `workflow_call` must be invoked by another workflow, and a workflow-level environment value is not a required user input. See [GitHub workflow events](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/workflows-and-actions/events-that-trigger-workflows).
